narrative: FY2023-24 is the most differentiated year. Tata and JSW remained the benchmark with 100% across all four metrics. SAIL improved employee skill coverage to 50.04% and employee H&S to 32.21%, but still trailed materially and continued to report no worker skill-upgradation value. Jindal Stainless showed a major coverage break: employee H&S at 50.04%, employee skill at 32.21%, worker H&S at 19.27%, and worker skill at 3.73%. The Jindal movement is likely a reporting-boundary, classification or programme-capture issue rather than a structural absence of safety systems, because its later FY2024-25 BRSR describes formal HIRA, HAZOP, JSA, work permits, toolbox training and a Safety Training Centre. Tata’s FY2023-24 disclosure is especially mature because it links 100% coverage to a structured development architecture: foundational training, functional and technical capability, business and leadership skills, digital, sustainability and agility, and worker-specific capability-building models.
